original_abstract:
The rapid proliferation of large language models (LLMs), such as ChatGPT, has introduced significant challenges to academic integrity, prompting a demand for technical solutions to identify AI-generated content. This study evaluates the efficacy and reliability of 14 different detection tools—including 12 publicly available free versions and two commercial systems (Turnitin and PlagiarismCheck)—using a rigorous methodology and an original dataset across various academic disciplines. The researchers focused on five primary scenarios: human-written text, machine-translated human text, raw AI-generated content, and AI-generated content modified through manual editing or automated paraphrasing (obfuscation).

Our findings indicate that none of the tested tools are currently reliable or accurate enough to be used as definitive evidence in academic misconduct investigations. While tools generally performed better at identifying human-authored text, they exhibited a persistent bias toward classifying content as human-written, leading to high rates of false negatives (undetected AI text). Furthermore, the accuracy of these detectors plummeted when subjected to machine translation or obfuscation techniques, with machine-paraphrased AI content frequently bypassing detection entirely. Given these limitations and the risk of false accusations—particularly for students using translation software—the paper concludes that institutions should prioritize preventive pedagogical strategies and redesigned assessments over a reliance on flawed detection technologies.
humanized_abstract:
The surge in popularity of large language models (LLMs) like ChatGPT and the speed with which they are proliferating have upped the ante for maintaining academic integrity. This necessitates the need for technological tools to detect AI generated content. In this research study, the validity of 14 distinct detection tools that were examined – twelve were free versions sourced from the open domain and two were commercial plugins namely Turnitin and PlagiarismCheck – employing a carefully articulated methodology and bespoke dataset crafted to cover a broad spectrum of subject areas across the academia. The validation exercise was carried out on five scenarios namely: i) human written text, ii) human generated text which was machine translated, iii) direct output of AI, and iv) AI generated text which is then altered by means of human input or by employing a paraphrasing application (obfuscation).

These findings indicate that that currently none of the plugins tested constitute reliable or proof of human authorship in cases of academic misconduct investigations. Overall the plugins that had more success with identifying human written content, also had a bias to mark nearly all of the samples as such, resulting in false negatives of AI written content, while being somewhat ineffective with regards to identifying machine translated or obfuscated content, particularly with regards to the machine paraphrased content produced by certain generators that often slipped by unmarked as AI written. It is recommended that educational institutions are to spend more efforts on pedagogical measures for preventing academic misconduct in conjunction with the design of tests and quizzes, as the current state of affairs lends itself to false allegations being brought against an innocent student using legitimate tools to assist them with their studies.
